1.1: arche tou euangeliou Iesou Christou huiou theou — the beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ Son of God — Mark's opening is not a genealogy or birth narrative but a simple proclamation-header: the subject of what follows is the euangelion (good news, the Roman herald's announcement of a great event — usually a military victory or an emperor's birth). Arche (beginning) echoes Gen 1:1 and John 1:1; Mark presents the ministry of Jesus as the beginning of the new creation's decisive chapter. The genitive Iesou Christou is objective (the gospel about Jesus Christ) and possibly subjective (the gospel Jesus proclaimed); both are true.
1.15: peplerothe ho kairos — the time is fulfilled — kairos (appointed time, season) vs. chronos (linear time); the kairos is the divinely appointed moment within time when the decisive event occurs. euthys (immediately) is Mark's signature adverb, appearing 41 times. It gives Mark's narrative the pace of urgent eschatological action: the kingdom's arrival is not gradual but sudden and demanding. metanoeite kai pisteuete en to euangelio — repent and believe in the good news — the two responses named: metanoeite (change direction) + pisteuete (trust, commit); together they define the covenant entry into the kingdom.
1.44: ora medeni meden eipes — see that you say nothing to anyone — the first of Mark's Messianic Secret commands (cf. 1:25, 3:12, 5:43, 7:36, 8:30, 9:9). The Messianic Secret is Mark's distinctive theological pattern: Jesus repeatedly silences those who recognize him. This is not deception but the correct ordering of revelation — the full meaning of his identity can only be understood through the cross, which has not yet occurred. To announce the Messiah before the cross would be to proclaim a false Messiah (a triumphalist king rather than the Suffering Servant).
1.2: Kathos gegraptai en to Esaia to prophete — as it is written in Isaiah the prophet — the textual crux: what follows is a composite of Mal 3:1 (idou apostellO ton angelon mou, v.2b) and Isa 40:3 (v.3), yet the attribution names only Isaiah. Some manuscripts read "in the prophets" (en tois prophetais) to resolve the problem, but the earliest witnesses (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) read "Isaiah the prophet." Mark headlines Isaiah as the dominant interpretive frame — Isa 40 opens the new-exodus prophecy of comfort, and the Malachi forerunner motif is absorbed under it. Both the messenger (Mal 3:1) and the voice (Isa 40:3) are read as the single figure John the Baptist.
1.3: Phone boOntos en te eremo — the voice of one crying in the wilderness — Isa 40:3 LXX. The MT reads "in the wilderness prepare the way of the LORD," with the wilderness modifying the road to be prepared; the LXX (followed by all four Gospels) re-punctuates so that the voice is itself in the wilderness: "a voice crying in the wilderness." This makes the wilderness the location of the herald, not merely the destination of the road. Hetoimasate ten hodon kyriou — prepare the way of the Lord — in Isa 40 the kyrios is YHWH; the Gospels apply the title to Jesus, one of the most direct Lord = Jesus = YHWH equations in the NT, positioned at the Gospel's very opening.
1.4: Baptisma metanoias eis aphesin hamartiOn — a baptism of repentance for/toward the forgiveness of sins — three theologically dense terms. Baptisma had precedents in Jewish proselyte immersion and Qumran practice but John's is distinctive: once-for-all, administered rather than self-immersion, linked to metanoia. Metanoia — repentance, change of mind and direction — is the whole-person turning. Aphesis hamartiOn — forgiveness/release of sins — the noun is the cognate of aphiemi (to release, forgive), used repeatedly in 2:5,7,9,10 for the paralytic's forgiveness: John's baptism promises release; Jesus accomplishes it by his own authority.
1.5: Pasa he Ioudaia chOra kai hoi Ierosolymitai pantes — the whole Judean countryside and all the Jerusalemites — the double universalizing expression (pasa ... pantes) is Markan hyperbole for massive popular response, setting up the contrast with the religious authorities who will resist. Exomologoumenoi tas hamartias autOn — confessing their sins — the present participle indicates ongoing, iterative confession accompanying each individual baptism. Public verbal acknowledgment of sin is the marker of the genuine metanoia that John's baptism of repentance presupposes.
1.6: Endedymenos trichas kamelou kai zOnen dermatinen peri ten osphyn autou — clothed in camel hair with a leather belt around his waist — the description echoes 2 Kgs 1:8 LXX, where the same phrase (zOne dermatine, leather belt) identifies Elijah the Tishbite to King Ahaziah. Mark's readers familiar with the LXX recognize the Elijah-figure before the explicit identification at 9:11-13. AkOrides kai meli agrion — locusts and wild honey — the wilderness diet: locusts are permitted insects (Lev 11:22) and honey from the wild; the diet of one outside the agricultural-economic system signals prophetic vocation and ascetic calling.
1.7: Ho ischyroteros mou opisO mou erchetai — the one stronger than I is coming after me — ischyroteros (comparative of ischyros, strong/powerful) is the key Christological term: not merely "greater" in honor but stronger in power. The word anticipates the strong-man saying of 3:27 where Jesus binds the strong man to plunder his house — the coming one is stronger than the adversary John's language will later frame. Hou ouk eimi hikanos kypsas lysai ton himanta tOn hypodematOn autou — of whom I am not worthy, having stooped down, to untie the strap of his sandals — the most servile household task (sandal-removal by a slave) is what John considers beyond his appropriate role in relation to the coming one.
1.8: Autos de baptisei hymas en pneumati hagiO — he will baptize you in the Holy Spirit — the contrast is precise: John's medium is water; the coming one's medium is Holy Spirit. Greek lacks the article before pneumati hagiO here, emphasizing the quality/character of the spirit rather than purely personal identity, though anarthrousness alone is not decisive. The preposition en (in/with) governs both: baptism in water (v.5) and baptism in Holy Spirit — same preposition, different media. The Spirit baptism draws on Isa 44:3; Ezek 36:25-27; Joel 2:28-29 — the eschatological outpouring of divine presence.
1.9: Elthen Iesous apo Nazaret tes Galilaias — Jesus came from Nazareth of Galilee — the first appearance of Jesus in Mark's narrative: no birth, no genealogy, no infancy. Mark begins with the adult Jesus arriving for baptism. Nazareth was a small village (perhaps 400 residents) in the Galilean hills, socially unremarkable (cf. John 1:46). The geographic identification (apo Nazaret tes Galilaias) names his origin for those who will later question him (6:1-6). Ebaptisthe eis ton Iordanen hypo IOannou — was baptized in the Jordan by John — the passive emphasizes John's agency: Jesus submits to John's baptism rather than administering one.
1.10: Schizomenous tous ouranous — the heavens being torn — the verb schizO (to split, tear, cleave) is more forceful than Matthew's and Luke's anoigO (to open). Isa 64:1 (MT) uses qara' (tear) for the same longed-for divine advent: "Oh that you would tear the heavens and come down!" Mark's verb enacts the Isaianic prayer more vividly: the heavens are not gently parted but violently split. The same verb schizO appears at 15:38 for the temple curtain torn at the crucifixion — a deliberate structural inclusio: heavens torn at the Spirit's descent, curtain torn at Jesus's death.
1.11: Sy ei ho huios mou ho agapetos — you are my beloved Son — the address is second person (sy), directly to Jesus, unlike Matthew's third-person announcement to bystanders. Ho agapetos (the beloved) carries the force of the Hebrew yahid (only, unique) — the love of the only son (cf. Gen 22:2 LXX where agapetos translates yahid for Isaac). The voice combines Ps 2:7 ("you are my Son") and Isa 42:1 ("my servant in whom my soul delights") — the royal psalm and the servant song. The dual citation at the baptism frames the entire Markan narrative: Son of God who is simultaneously Suffering Servant.
1.12: Kai euthys to pneuma auton ekballei eis ten eremon — and immediately the Spirit drives him out into the wilderness — euthys (immediately) is the signature Markan adverb, appearing ~40 times. Ekballei (present tense indicative: drives out, expels) is the same verb used throughout Mark for casting out demons: the Spirit's action on Jesus employs the exorcism vocabulary rather than Matthew's gentler "led" (anechthe, 4:1). The Spirit who descended like a dove acts with the force of expulsion. The divine compulsion toward the messianic mission begins at once and without pause.
1.13: Peirazomenos hypo tou Satana — being tempted by Satan — the present participle indicates ongoing temptation throughout the forty days, not a single sequence of three discrete tests as in Matthew and Luke. Mark gives no content to the temptations. En meta tOn theriOn — he was with the wild animals — unique to Mark, the detail has been read as Edenic restoration (Adam naming animals, Gen 2:19-20), as the new-creation peace of Isa 11:6-9; 65:25, or simply as wilderness desolation and danger. The angels attending him (diekonoun autO) provide the counterpoint: serviced by angels while threatened by the adversary and wild creation.
1.14: Meta de to paradothenai ton IOannen — after John was delivered/handed over — the verb paradothenai (aorist passive of paradidOmi) is the passion verb throughout Mark: it will be used for Jesus's own betrayal and delivery to death (9:31; 10:33; 14:10,11,18,21,41,42,44; 15:1,10,15). Its first appearance is for John: the one who preceded Jesus in ministry and proclamation precedes him in being handed over. The passive voice implies divine purpose working through human agency. Euangelion tou theou — the gospel of God — the genitive tou theou: God as the source of the good news, not merely its subject.
1.16: Paragon para ten thalassan tes Galilaias — passing alongside the Sea of Galilee — the participle paragon (passing by, traveling alongside) is casual: Jesus is in purposeful motion, not positioned at a call-station. The calling comes in the course of movement. Amphiballontas en te thalasse — casting (nets) into the sea — the present participle of amphiballO (to cast around on both sides, a circular casting-net technique) describes the brothers at their trade, unambiguously working when called. The call interrupts labor, not leisure.
1.17: Deute opisO mou kai poieso hymas genesthai halieis anthrOpOn — come after me and I will make you become fishers of people — the construction poieso hymas genesthai (I will make you become) is emphatic: Jesus will be the agent of transformation. Opiso mou (behind/after me) is the discipleship-direction idiom: students walked behind their teacher. Halieis anthrOpOn — fishers of people — the vocational image extends: the skill-set changes while the activity-structure (casting, gathering) continues in a new domain. The Jer 16:16 echo (fishers gathering Israel for judgment) is reinterpreted toward gathering for the kingdom.
1.18: Kai euthys aphentes ta diktya ekolouthEsan autO — and immediately, leaving their nets, they followed him — the three elements compress the call-response: euthys (immediately), aphentes (having abandoned — aorist participle of aphiemi, the leave/release/forgive verb), ekolouthEsan (followed — aorist). The nets are left mid-cast, not stowed; the abandonment is total and unreflective. Mark presents the call as carrying the imperatival force of a divine summons that admits no deliberation.
1.19: Iakōbon ton tou Zebedaiou kai IOannEn ton adelphon autou — James the son of Zebedee and John his brother — James is consistently named first, consistent with his prominence in the early Jerusalem community (Acts 12:2). Both are identified by patronym (tou Zebedaiou) before by fraternal relationship, locating them in a family business. Katartizontas ta diktya — mending/preparing their nets — katartizO (to fit together, restore to proper condition, mend) describes routine net maintenance, the background work of a fishing operation. They are interrupted in preparatory work, not in active fishing.
1.20: Kai euthys ekalesen autous — and immediately he called them — the same pattern as vv.17-18: immediate call, immediate response with no intervening discussion. Aphentes ton patera autOn Zebedaion en tO ploiO meta tOn misthiOn — leaving their father Zebedee in the boat with the hired men — the mention of hired men (misthioi) suggests a commercially viable enterprise rather than subsistence fishing. James and John leave not only their father but economic stability. Mark records neither Zebedee's reaction nor any farewell; the absoluteness of the departure is the point.
1.21: Eisporeuontai eis Kapharnaoum — they entered Capernaum — the historic present (eisporeuontai) is characteristic Markan narrative vivid-tense; it gives the account an eyewitness immediacy. Capernaum (Tell Hum, northwest shore of the Sea of Galilee) becomes Jesus's Galilean base in Mark 1-6. Tois sabbasin eis ten synagOgen eiselthOn edidasken — on the Sabbath, having entered the synagogue, he was teaching — the imperfect edidasken indicates sustained activity. Jesus enters the existing institutional structure (the synagogue as communal assembly and Torah study center) on the Sabbath (maximum attendance day).
1.22: Exeplessonto epi te didache autou — they were astonished/struck out at his teaching — ekplesso (to strike out, astonish — ek-prefix intensifies) is strong: the teaching struck them out of their existing framework. En gar didaskOn autous hos exousian echOn kai ouch hos hoi grammateis — for he was teaching them as one having authority, and not as the scribes — the contrast is between exousia (authority: inherent, primary, self-possessed) and the scribal method of citing received tradition ("Rabbi X says"). The scribes taught with derived authority; Jesus taught with primary authority. The crowd recognizes the formal difference before they can articulate its theological basis.
1.23: En synagOge autOn anthrOpos en pneumati akathartO — in their synagogue a man in an unclean spirit — en pneumati akathartO (in an unclean spirit) is ambiguous: the man inhabits or is inhabited by the spirit — the preposition en denotes the sphere of influence. Akathartos (unclean) is the ritual-purity vocabulary of Leviticus 13-15 applied to a spirit: not merely a moral category but a cultic one, making the exorcism in the synagogue on the Sabbath simultaneously a power confrontation and a purity restoration.
1.24: Ti hemin kai soi, IEsou NazarEne — what is there between us and you, Jesus of Nazareth — ti hemin kai soi is a rendering of the Hebrew idiom mah-li wlak (2 Sam 16:10; 1 Kgs 17:18): "what business do we have together?" or "do not interfere with us." The plural (hemin = us) reflects either a genuine plurality of possessing spirits or a collective of demonic forces. Ho hagios tou theou — the Holy One of God — the demon's title is the precise antonym of its own nature (akathartos = unclean): the Holy One confronts the unclean spirit in the sacred space of the synagogue, a triple contrast of holiness-uncleanness in the place of holiness.
1.25: PhimOthEti kai exelthe ex autou — be muzzled and come out of him — phimoo (to muzzle, silence with a muzzle) is used of gagging working animals (Deut 25:4 LXX; 1 Cor 9:9) and of silencing human adversaries (Matt 22:34; 1 Pet 2:15). Jesus silences the demon before expelling it — the Messianic Secret begins with the forcible muzzling of demonic testimony, even accurate testimony about Jesus's identity. The two-part command addresses both speech (muzzle!) and presence (come out!), removing both the demonic voice and the demonic habitation.
1.26: Kai sparaxan auton to pneuma to akatharton kai phOnEsan phOne megale exElthen ex autou — and having convulsed him, the unclean spirit, having cried out with a great voice, came out of him — the departure is violent (sparaxan = having convulsed, shaken violently) and audible (phOnEsan phOne megale = crying with a great voice). The violence demonstrates the exorcism's reality: the spirit is genuinely expelled against its resistance, not merely suppressed. The audible cry signals defeat. Mark preserves the phenomenological drama — Jesus's authority is exercised against genuine demonic opposition, not a ceremonial performance.
1.27: Didache kaine kat' exousian — new teaching according to authority — the crowd's synthesis of what they have witnessed: a kaine didache (new teaching; kainos = new in quality and kind, not merely recent) exercised kat' exousian (in the mode of authority). The exorcism and the teaching are not separated — the same authority that distinguished the teaching of v.22 has now expelled a demon. Kai tois pneumasin tois akathartois epitassei, kai hypakouousin autO — he commands even the unclean spirits and they obey him — epitassO (to assign over, command authoritatively) + hypakouO (to hear under, to comply): military chain-of-command vocabulary applied to the spiritual realm.
1.28: Exelthen hE akoe autou euthys pantachou eis holen ten periochon tes Galilaias — and immediately the report about him went out everywhere into the whole region of Galilee — euthys again; fame spreads with the same immediacy as the call responses and the exorcism. AkoE (hearing, report — from akouO) — the medium of fame is hearing-and-telling: what is heard is passed on. The geographic spread (pantachou ... holen ten periochon = everywhere ... the whole surrounding region) establishes that the Capernaum synagogue incident inaugurates the regional reputation that will drive the narrative's crowd dynamics in subsequent chapters.
1.29: Kai euthys ek tes synagOgEs exelthontes Elthan eis ten oikian SimOnos kai Andreou — and immediately, going out of the synagogue, they entered the house of Simon and Andrew — euthys again; the domestic miracle immediately follows the public one. The house of Simon and Andrew in Capernaum is the domestic base for the early ministry; archaeological remains beneath the Byzantine church at Capernaum are identified by early pilgrims with Peter's house. The transition from synagogue to house moves from the institutional to the private, extending the authority of v.22-27 into the domestic sphere.
1.30: He de penthera SimOnos katekeito pyressousa — the mother-in-law of Simon was lying down with a fever — pethera (mother-in-law) establishes that Simon is married before his call to discipleship, background to 1 Cor 9:5 where Paul mentions Peter traveling with a wife. PyressO (to be feverish) is a medical term; Luke 4:38 specifies "a great fever." Euthys legousin autO peri autes — immediately they spoke to him about her — the word euthys appears again; those with access to the healer immediately intercede for those who need him. The natural intercessory impulse is validated and answered at once.
1.31: Kai proselthOn Egeiren auten kratEsas tes cheiros — and approaching, he raised her up, having grasped her hand — egeirO (to raise up) is the resurrection verb used for a healing: Jesus raises the feverish woman as he will raise the dead girl (5:41) and as he will be raised (16:6). The physical touch (kratEsas tes cheiros, grasping the hand) reverses the purity logic: contact with the sick normally transmits impurity; Jesus reverses the flow. AphEken auten ho pyretos — the fever left her — the fever is described as a subject that "leaves," like a spirit departing (same verb aphiemi). DiEkonei autois — she was serving them — immediate grateful service in the imperfect: the healed life restored to purposeful activity.
1.32: Opsias de genomenes, hote edysen ho helios — when evening came, when the sun had set — the double temporal marker is precise: people waited until the Sabbath ended (sunset Saturday) to bring the sick, since carrying them constituted prohibited Sabbath labor. The careful notation reveals a Torah-observant community: the crowds waited for halakhic permission before acting on the healing-fame of v.28. Tous kakOs echontas kai tous daimonizomenous — those faring badly (sick) and those being demonized — Mark's two categories of affliction; both are addressed in the evening ministry that follows.
1.33: Kai hE polis holE episynEgmene pros ten thyran — and the whole city had gathered at the door — the hyperbolic hE polis holE (the whole city, everyone) matches the pasa hE Ioudaia chOra of v.5. The gathering at the door (epi ten thyran) is spatially vivid: the private house becomes the site of what was hoped for in the temple — access to the divine presence that heals. The contrast with v.35 (Jesus rising before dawn to withdraw for prayer) sets up the tension between the crowd's demand and Jesus's self-directed mission-agenda.
1.34: Kai ouk Ephien lalein ta daimonia, hoti Edeisan auton — and he did not permit the demons to speak, because they knew him — Edeisan auton (they knew him): the imperfect of oida (to know). The demons' knowledge is ontological recognition, not acquired information; they perceive who Jesus is in a way that the human characters do not yet grasp. The silencing of demonic testimony (ouk Ephien lalein) is the second Messianic-Secret instance after v.25. Jesus suppresses accurate identification through unauthorized channels — the correct channel for Christological disclosure is the cross and resurrection, not demonic proclamation.
1.35: Kai proi ennucha lian anastas exElthen kai apElthen eis eremon topon kakei prosEucheto — and very early while still night, having risen up, he went out and departed into a desolate place, and there he was praying — three temporal words pile up: proi (early), ennucha (while night), lian (exceedingly). The prayer precedes the day's work, in absolute predawn darkness. Anastas (having risen up) is the resurrection verb applied to rising from sleep; the pattern of predawn prayer recurs at 6:46 and 14:32-42. The eremos topos (desolate/desert place) echoes vv.3-4: the wilderness is the place of divine encounter, not merely absence and threat.
1.36: Kai katedioxen auton SimOn kai hoi met' autou — and Simon and those with him searched him out/pursued him — katadiOkO (to pursue, hunt down — the kata prefix intensifies) is a strong word for seeking: they hunted him rather than simply looked. The urgency implies the disciples' agenda: return to the crowd, capitalize on the popularity. Simon is named first, establishing his leadership role among the first four disciples. The scene sets up the tension between the disciples' crowd-driven logic and Jesus's mission-driven countermovement in v.38.
1.37: Pantes zEtousin se — everyone is seeking you — the disciples' report is an implicit call to return: demand is high, opportunity is ripe. But zEtousin (seeking, present indicative) in Mark rarely carries positive connotations: the same verb is used of those who seek to kill Jesus (11:18; 12:12; 14:1,11). The crowd seeks Jesus for healing; Jesus redirects away from meeting demand toward fulfilling mission. The disciples' assumption — popularity equals success — will be corrected throughout Mark.
1.38: Agomen allachou eis tas echomenas kOmopoleis hina kakei kEryxO — let us go elsewhere into the neighboring market-towns so that I may preach there also — kOmopoleis (town-villages, market-towns — NT hapax, combining kOmE, village, and polis, city) describes the semi-urban market centers of Galilee. Eis touto gar exElthon — for to this end I went out — exElthon (I went out) is grammatically ambiguous: proximately "went out from the house/Capernaum," but possibly carrying the theological sense of "came out from the Father" that John's Gospel articulates explicitly. The double resonance may be intentional in Mark's compact narration.
1.39: Kai Elthen kErysson eis tas synagOgas autOn eis holen ten Galilaian kai ta daimonia ekballOn — and he went proclaiming in their synagogues into all Galilee and casting out demons — a summary statement of the early Galilean ministry, pairing the two activities: kEryssO (proclaim, herald) in synagogues and ekballO daimonia (cast out demons). The itinerant nature (holen ten Galilaian = all Galilee) and the synagogue-institution focus establish the Jewish-communal context of the mission. The regional exorcisms extend the Capernaum synagogue incident into a Galilee-wide pattern.
1.40: Kai erchetai pros auton lepros parakalOn auton kai gonypetOn — and a leper came to him, imploring him, kneeling — lepros in the NT covers the range of skin conditions under the Hebrew sara'at (Lev 13-14), not exclusively Hansen's disease. The legal consequence was communal exclusion: calling out "unclean!" and living apart (Lev 13:45-46). This man approaches Jesus in direct violation of the protocol. GonypetOn (kneeling) is formal supplication. Ean thelEs dynasai me katharisai — if you are willing, you are able to cleanse me — the question is not about Jesus's power (dynasai = you can, affirmed) but his will (theleis): the man is uncertain whether the healer will choose to act on his behalf.
1.41: Kai splagchnistheis ektinas ten cheira Hepsato — and having been moved with compassion, stretching out his hand he touched — the major textual variant: Codex Bezae (D) and some Old Latin witnesses read orgistheis (having been angered) rather than splagchnistheis (having been moved with compassion). The harder-reading principle favors orgistheis — scribes were more likely to soften "anger" to "compassion" than vice versa — and some scholars argue Jesus's anger was at the disease, at the man's forced exclusion, or at the demonic affliction. The MKT follows the majority text with splagchnistheis. The verb splagchnizomai (from splagchna, viscera) describes visceral, gut-level compassion rather than surface sympathy.
1.42: Euthys apElthen ap' autou hE lepra kai ekatharisthE — immediately the leprosy left him and he was cleansed — euthys; the instantaneous healing is described in two parallel clauses: the disease left (apElthen, same verb for spirits departing) and the person was cleansed (katharizO, the ritual-purity verb used throughout this pericope at vv.40,41,42,44). Both the medical and the ritual dimension are restored simultaneously. The language of cleansing (katharos/katharizO) connects directly to Leviticus 13-14, whose cleansing-declaration process Jesus redirects back to its institutional channel in v.44.
1.43: Kai embrimEsamenos autO euthys exebalen auton — and having strongly warned him he immediately sent him away — embrimaomai (from a root meaning to snort like a horse, hence: to groan strongly, charge sternly, be emotionally moved) appears in John 11:33,38 for Jesus's deep agitation at Lazarus's tomb. The word carries strong emotional charge beyond a merely verbal warning. ExebalenTon (he sent him away, drove him out — the exorcism verb) is striking: the same word used for casting out demons is used for sending this man away with urgency, reflecting the forcefulness of the command not to speak.
1.45: Ho de exelthOn Erxato kEryssein polla kai diaphEmizein ton logon — but going out he began to proclaim many things and spread the word widely — the healed man disobeys the silence command (v.44): diaphEmizO (to spread abroad, broadcast) is the opposite of the commanded silence. HOste mEketi auton dynasthai phanerOs eis polin eiselthein — so that he could no longer openly enter a city — the disobedience has strategic consequences: Jesus's urban ministry is compromised by the resulting notoriety, forcing him to the desert places (erEmois topois). The irony closes the chapter: the healed outcast goes publicly where he was excluded; the healer who came publicly must now remain in the lonely places where the outcast formerly lived.

2.10: exousian echei ho huios tou anthropou aphienai hamartias epi tes ges — the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins — the first Danielic Son of Man claim in Mark (cf. Dan 7:13-14); the universal dominion given to the Son of Man in Daniel is now being exercised in the specific act of pronouncing forgiveness in a Capernaum house. Epi tes ges (on earth) is emphatic: this divine prerogative is being exercised in the present earthly ministry, not deferred to the final judgment. The visible healing is the credential of the invisible forgiveness.
2.19: mhe dynastai hoi huioi tou nymphonos en ho ho nymphios estin autois nesteuen — the wedding guests cannot fast while the bridegroom is with them — huioi tou nymphonos (sons of the wedding chamber) is a Semitic idiom for wedding guests; the idiom places Jesus in the role of the messianic bridegroom (cf. Isa 54:5-6, Hos 2:19). The presence of the bridegroom defines the appropriate response: feasting, not fasting. The eschatological joy is already present in Jesus's person; mourning practices are inappropriate in the kingdom's inaugural moment.
2.1: Kai eiselthOn palin eis Kapharnaoum di' hemErOn — and having entered again into Capernaum after some days — di' hemErOn (through days, after an interval of days) signals resumption of Capernaum-based ministry after the regional circuit of 1:39-45. Ekousthē hoti en oikO estin — it was heard that he was at home — en oikO (at home, in a house) may mean Simon's house (1:29-31) or a dwelling Jesus used regularly in Capernaum; the definite article's absence leaves both open. The spread of the report (ekousthE = it was heard, passive) without explicit announcement is consistent with the reputation-spreading dynamic of 1:28.
2.2: Kai synagEchthEsan polloi hOste mEketi chOrein mEde ta pros ten thyran — and many gathered so that there was no longer room even at the door — the spatial overflow repeats the pattern of 1:33 (the whole city at the door). Kai elalei autois ton logon — and he was speaking the word to them — ton logon (the word) without specification: early Christian usage of logos as a technical term for the gospel message is operative. Mark's narrative compresses the crowd scene into one clause and moves immediately to the rooftop drama — the preaching is background to the healing.
2.3: Kai erchontai pherein pros auton paralytikon airomenon hypo tessarOn — and they came bringing to him a paralyzed man carried by four — paralytikos (from paraluO = to loosen beside, to unloose the motor faculty) is the clinical term for motor incapacity. The specification of four carriers (hypo tessarOn) is precise and picturesque: the roof operation requires four people at the corners of the mat. The verb pherO (to carry, bear — also to bring an offering) creates the image of an offering of need being presented to the healer.
2.4: Kai mE dynamis prosengisai autO dia ton ochlon apestegasan ten stegen hopou En — and not being able to bring him near because of the crowd, they unroofed the roof where he was — apestegasan ten stegen (they unroofed the roof) is the NT hapax verb apostegazO. A Palestinian peasant roof was typically packed earth on wooden beams or interlaid branches, accessible by an outside staircase. Opening it required deliberate demolition: removing material to create a hole large enough for the mat. Exoruxantes chalaOsin ton krabbaton — having dug through they lowered the mat — krabbatos (pallet, cot — low-register word for a simple sleeping mat) marks the social status of the man being lowered.
2.5: IdOn de ho Iesous ten pistin autOn — seeing their faith — the object of idOn (having seen) is ten pistin autOn (their faith): what Jesus sees is the visible, enacted faith of those who demolished a roof. Faith in Mark is demonstrated through action rather than verbal confession; the observable expression of faith (the physical effort) is what Jesus perceives. Teknon, aphiontai sou hai hamartiai — child, your sins are being forgiven — teknon (child, term of affection) is the intimate pastoral address. Aphiontai (present passive: are being forgiven, are being released) — the forgiveness is active in the present moment of speech, not a future promise.
2.6: Esan de tines tOn grammateOn ekei kathEmenoi kai dialogizomenoi en tais kardiais autOn — but there were some of the scribes sitting there and deliberating in their hearts — dialogizomai (to reason through, deliberate — dia + logos) carries the sense of working through a problem argument by argument. The scribes reason silently — en tais kardiais autOn (in their hearts), the seat of thought in Hebrew-Jewish anthropology. Their internal deliberation is private until Jesus reads it (v.8).
2.7: Ti houtos houtOs lalei? Blasphemei — why does this one speak thus? He blasphemes — blaspheO (to harm/injure the divine reputation) was punishable by death under Lev 24:16. The scribes' theological reasoning is internally coherent: tis dynatai aphienai hamartias ei me heis ho theos — who can forgive sins except one, God? The premise is correct (forgiveness is God's prerogative); the conclusion (therefore Jesus is blaspheming) fails only if Jesus is who Mark has already announced him to be (Son of God, 1:1). The passage's Christological argument turns precisely on that identification.
2.8: Kai euthys epignous ho Iesous tO pneumati autou hoti houtOs dialogizontai en heautois — and immediately, knowing by his spirit that thus they were deliberating within themselves — epiginOskO (to know fully/completely) in the aorist participle: immediate and complete perception. TO pneumati autou (by/in his spirit) — direct spiritual knowledge, not deductive inference. In OT theology, knowledge of the heart's contents belongs to God alone (Jer 17:9-10; Ps 139:1-4; 1 Sam 16:7). Jesus's perception of unspoken thoughts enacts a divine prerogative, making the verse a Christological argument embedded in the healing narrative.
2.9: Ti estin eukopOteron, eipein tO paralytikO, Aphiontai sou hai hamartiai, E eipein, Egeire kai aron ton krabbaton sou kai peripatei — which is easier, to say to the paralyzed man "your sins are being forgiven," or to say "rise and take your mat and walk"? — the rhetorical comparison (eukopOteron = easier) is a standard argument from the greater to the lesser. Pronouncing forgiveness is verbally unverifiable by human observers; commanding a paralyzed man to rise is immediately and publicly verifiable. If the verifiable thing is accomplished, the unverifiable claim is thereby vindicated. The question does not deny the charges but reframes them on verifiable grounds.
2.11: Soi legO, egeire aron ton krabbaton sou kai hypage eis ton oikon sou — I say to you, rise, take your mat and go to your house — the three imperatives address the paralyzed man directly: egeire (rise — the resurrection verb as a command before the restoration), aron (take up), hypage (go). The command asserts motor function before it has been demonstrated. The reversal encoded in aron ton krabbaton (carry the mat) is pointed: the man carried on his mat will carry the mat. He who was dependent will now bear his own means of dependence as a trophy of the healing.
2.12: Kai Egerthē kai euthys aras ton krabbaton exElthen emprosthen pantOn — and he was raised up and immediately, having taken up the mat, he went out before all — egerthE (was raised up, passive — the resurrection passive, the same form used at 16:6 for the resurrection of Jesus). The three actions mirror the three commands of v.11: he rose, he took the mat, he went. Oidamen hoti houtOs oudepote eidamen — we have never seen it thus — the historical "never before" marks the unprecedented character of what has occurred. The crowd's amazement (existasthai = to stand outside oneself, to be beside oneself with astonishment) is the collective witness to the Christological claim enacted in the healing.
2.13: Kai exElthen palin para ten thalassan — and he went out again alongside the sea — the spatial rhythm of Mark's narrative: crowded house → open lakeshore. Pas ho ochlos Ercheto pros auton, kai edidasken autous — all the crowd came to him and he was teaching them — the imperfect edidasken indicates sustained teaching. The lakeside setting (open, informal, accessible from any direction) contrasts with the synagogue (structured, institutional, weekly). Jesus uses both venues: the teaching authority of v.22 operates in both settings.
2.14: Leuin ton tou Alphaiou kathEmenon epi to telOnion — Levi the son of Alphaeus sitting at the tax booth — the tax/toll collector (telOnEs) at his station (telOnion, tax-booth, customs post) was typically positioned at a road, bridge, or city gate to levy tolls on goods. The identification "of Alphaeus" (tou Alphaiou) appears also at 3:18 for James son of Alphaeus, raising the question of fraternal relationship and identification with Matthew (Matt 9:9; 10:3). The call pattern is the same as 1:17-20: Jesus passing, seeing, two-word call, immediate compliance.
2.15: Polloi telOnai kai hamartOloi synanekeinto tO IEsou kai tois mathEtais autou — many tax collectors and sinners were reclining at table with Jesus and his disciples — synanakeimai (to recline with at table) is the technical vocabulary for table fellowship in the Greco-Roman meal posture. Eating with someone in antiquity declared social solidarity and, for Jews, implied ritual compatibility. HamartOloi (sinners) here likely designates those who did not observe Torah in the Pharisaic manner — particularly regarding tithing, purity, and sabbath — rather than generically immoral persons.
2.16: Hoi grammateis tOn PharisaiOi idontes hoti esthiei meta tOn hamartOlOn kai telOnOn elegon tois mathEtais autou — the scribes of the Pharisees seeing that he eats with sinners and tax collectors said to his disciples — "scribes of the Pharisees" is more specific than "scribes": Torah-scholars operating within the Pharisaic purity program. The challenge is directed at the disciples (tois mathEtais autou) rather than at Jesus — a rhetorical strategy to undermine the teacher through his students. The question is indirect: "why does he eat with tax collectors and sinners?" addressed to those who should be enforcing proper standards on their teacher.
2.17: Ou chreian echousin hoi ischyontes iatrou all' hoi kakOs echontes — those who are strong do not need a physician but those who are sick — the medical analogy is self-evident and irrefutable: physicians go where illness is. The force of the analogy reframes the table-fellowship charge: if he associates with sinners, it is because sinners are his practice, not because he endorses their condition. Ouk Elthon kalesai dikaious alla hamartOlous — I did not come to call the righteous but sinners — kalesai (to call) picks up the discipleship-call language of the chapter: the tax collector was "called" (v.14), and the mission is to call those who are sinners. The possible irony: those who consider themselves dikaious (righteous) have no perceived need of the physician and thus place themselves outside his reach.
2.18: Kai Esan hoi mathEtai IOannou kai hoi PharisaiOi nEsteuontes — and the disciples of John and the Pharisees were fasting — the combination of John's disciples and the Pharisees as co-fasting groups is notable: they are theologically distinct communities aligned on this specific practice. The fasting at issue is voluntary piety-fasting (Pharisaic practice: Monday and Thursday), not the Yom Kippur fast required by Torah. The question comes from unnamed questioners (erchontai kai legousin = they come and say), creating an open challenge rather than a named-group confrontation.
2.20: Eleusontai de hEmerai hotan aparthE ap' autOn ho nymphios, kai tote nEsteusousin en ekeinE tE hEmera — but days will come when the bridegroom will be taken away from them, and then they will fast on that day — aparthE (aorist passive subjunctive of apairO, to take away, lift off by force) is the first unambiguous passion prediction in Mark: the bridegroom will be taken away. The passive voice indicates violent removal rather than voluntary departure. Tote nEsteusousin (then they will fast) — fasting is reinterpreted as the practice of mourning and eschatological waiting appropriate to the period of the bridegroom's absence after his death, not appropriate to the presence-period of his earthly ministry.
2.21: Epiblēma rhakous agnaphou epirraptei epi himation palaion — no one sews a patch of unshrunk cloth on an old garment — agnaphos (unfulled, unshrunk — a- privative + gnaptO, to card or full cloth) is cloth that has not undergone the shrinking process; sewn to a worn garment it will pull away when washed, making the tear worse. Palaion (old, worn out — from palaioo, to make obsolete) is the key term for the old order. The metaphor establishes that the new content of the kingdom is qualitatively incompatible with the old wineskin/garment structures — not that the old was bad, but that the new cannot be contained within it.
2.22: Kai oudeis ballei oinon neon eis askous palaiOus — no one pours new wine into old wineskins — the second metaphor. The wine is neon (recently made, young, still fermenting) and the skins must be new to expand with it; old skins have lost their elasticity. Note the vocabulary distinction: v.21 uses agnaphos/kainos (new in kind); v.22 uses neos (new in time). Kainos (qualitatively new) and neos (recently made) are distinct: the kingdom is both qualitatively new (unprecedented in kind) and freshly inaugurated (recently arrived). Both dimensions require new structures — not patchwork accommodations within the existing system.
2.23: Kai egeneto auton en tois sabbasin paraporeuesthai dia tOn sporImOn — and it happened that on the Sabbath he was passing through the grain fields — paraporeuesthai (to travel alongside, pass through) is movement through fields, not active harvesting. The disciples' action (Erxanto hodon poiein tillontes tous stachyas = began to make a way plucking heads of grain) was permitted for hungry travelers by Deut 23:25, but the Pharisees classify it as reaping under the Sabbath restrictions. The controversy is not about Torah violation per se but about Pharisaic halakhah that expanded the Sabbath reaping-prohibition to cover gleaning. Jesus's counterargument operates on the level of hermeneutical principle, not merely exception.
2.24: Ide, ti poiousin tois sabbasin ho ouk exestin? — see, why do they do on the Sabbath what is not lawful? — exestin (it is lawful/permitted) is the halakhic permissibility verb. The Pharisees address Jesus rather than the disciples — the teacher is responsible for students' behavior. The challenge is not to the disciples' hunger but to Jesus's halakhic teaching: he is being charged with licensing unlawful behavior. Jesus responds not by arguing the exception but by challenging the premises: David overrode a more serious prohibition; the Sabbath was made for humanity; the Son of Man is its Lord.
2.25: Oudepote anegnOte ho epoiEsen Dauid, hote chreian eschen — have you never read what David did when he was in need — the appeal to scripture (ouk anegnOte = have you not read?) is Jesus's standard debate-opening (also 10:9; 12:10,26). The David precedent (1 Sam 21:1-6) establishes that chreian eschen (pressing need) overrode the purity restriction on the bread of the Presence. The argument is from precedent: if the anointed king's need overrode a specific ritual prohibition, the disciples' hunger can override a secondary Sabbath restriction derived from Torah by Pharisaic extension.
2.26: Epi Abiathar archiereos — in the days of Abiathar the high priest — the historical crux: 1 Sam 21:1-6 places the incident under Ahimelech, not Abiathar. Abiathar was Ahimelech's son, who became the prominent high priest of David's reign (2 Sam 15:35; 1 Kgs 2:26-27). Proposed explanations: (1) an early traditional slip, preserved by Mark; (2) the phrase means "in the section about Abiathar the high priest" (the more famous of the two, used to identify the scriptural passage); (3) the two priests may have shared the office temporarily. No fully satisfying resolution exists; the phrase is the most-discussed historical difficulty in Mark. Tous artous tEs prothEseOs (the bread of Presentation/Setting-Out) is the showbread of Lev 24:5-9, restricted to Aaronic priests.
2.27: To sabbaton dia ton anthropon egeneto kai ouch ho anthrOpos dia to sabbaton — the Sabbath came into being for the sake of humanity and not humanity for the sake of the Sabbath — this verse is unique to Mark; neither Matthew nor Luke includes it, moving directly to v.28. Dia ton anthropon (for the sake of humanity/the human being) is the teleological statement: the Sabbath was created as a gift and provision (Gen 2:1-3; Exod 20:8-11; Deut 5:12-15), not a burdensome institution. The principle reverses the Pharisaic trajectory: they had made human beings serve the Sabbath-institution; Jesus restores the Sabbath to its intended direction of service toward humanity.
2.28: HOste kyrios estin ho huios tou anthrOpou kai tou sabbatou — so the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath — the argument's climax: kyrios (lord, master, owner) of the Sabbath belongs to the Son of Man. The kai (even) is emphatic: of the Sabbath itself, the institution that God established in creation. The Son of Man language carries the Dan 7:13-14 resonances of the one given universal authority and dominion. The claim moves from humanitarian argument (v.27: Sabbath for humanity) to Christological claim (v.28: the Son of Man is its Lord), making the final word of the chapter a direct assertion of Messianic authority over the Sabbath's meaning.

3.28: amēn lego hymin hoti panta aphethesetai tois huiois ton anthropon ta hamartemata kai hai blasphemiai — all sins will be forgiven the sons of men and whatever blasphemies they utter — the universality of forgiveness is stated first; then the single exception is given. blasphemia eis to pneuma to hagion — blasphemy against the Holy Spirit — the attribution of the Spirit's works (exorcism by the Spirit of God) to the demonic; the sin that cannot be forgiven because it involves the willful inversion of the Spirit's identification of Christ, thereby placing oneself permanently outside the Spirit's reach who grants repentance.
3.1: Eiselthen eis ten synagOgen, kai En ekei anthrOpos exErammenen echOn ten cheira — he entered again into the synagogue, and there was a man who had a withered hand — exErammenE (withered, dried up — from xerainO, to dry out) is the Greek medical term for a limb that has lost function through atrophy or nerve damage. The synagogue setting repeats the pattern of 1:21-28: the place of Torah instruction becomes the arena of messianic authority. The unnamed man's condition is publicly visible, making the Sabbath dilemma equally public.
3.2: Kai paretEroun auton ei tois sabbasin therapeusei auton, hina katEgorEsOsin autou — and they were watching him closely to see if he would heal him on the Sabbath, in order that they might accuse him — paretEroun (imperfect of paratEreO, to watch beside, observe closely, lie in wait) is legal surveillance: they are gathering evidence for a formal accusation (katEgorEsOsin, legal charge). The purpose clause (hina + subjunctive) makes the intent explicit: the healing they are about to witness is pre-interpreted as potential grounds for prosecution. The Sabbath healing is being set up as a legal trap.
3.3: Kai legei tO anthrOpO tO exErammenen ten cheira — and he said to the man who had the withered hand — Jesus addresses the man directly before resolving the legal question, staging the healing as a public event (egeire eis to meson = rise into the middle). The command to stand in the center (meson) removes the man from obscurity and makes the healing fully visible to everyone in the synagogue. There will be no ambiguity about what happens or who does it.
3.4: Exestin tois sabbasin agathon poiesai E kakOpoiesai, psychen sOsai E apokteinai? — is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good or to do evil, to save life or to kill? — the antithesis is sharp: doing good vs. doing evil, saving life vs. killing. Jesus frames the question so that refusing to heal is itself a moral act — inaction on behalf of suffering is not a neutral Sabbath posture but a form of evil. Hoi de esiOpOn — but they remained silent — the silence is not reverence but legal strategy: answering either way is a trap. Their silence confirms that they have no principled answer; they are not engaged in genuine halakhic debate but in evidence-gathering.
3.5: Kai periblepsamenos autous met' orgEs, syllypUmenos epi te pOrOsei tes kardias autOn — and looking around at them with anger, grieved at the hardness of their hearts — the dual emotional state is unique in the Gospels: orge (anger, righteous fury — same word as Isa 66:15; Rom 1:18 for divine wrath) and syllypoumenos (co-grieved, deeply distressed — the syn prefix adds the communal dimension: he shares in their suffering). Jesus is angry at the hardness and simultaneously grieved by it. The heart-hardness (pOrOsis tes kardias — pOrOsis = petrification, callusing) is the same condition described in Mark 6:52 (disciples after the feeding) and 8:17 — a recurring Markan diagnosis.
3.6: Kai exelthontes hoi PharisaiOi euthys meta tOn HErOdianOn symBoulion edidoun kat' autou hopOs auton apolEsOsin — and the Pharisees immediately went out and took counsel with the Herodians against him, how they might destroy him — euthys; the immediate consultation with the Herodians is surprising: the Pharisees (a purity-observance movement) and the Herodians (a political party supporting the Herodian dynasty) had conflicting agendas and mutual suspicion. Their alliance against Jesus is the political equivalent of the Sadducee-Pharisee alliance in 12:13-17: opposition to Jesus unites otherwise incompatible groups. ApolEsOsin (destroy) — this is the earliest death-plot in Mark, provoked by a healing.
3.7: Kai ho IEsous meta tOn mathEtOn autou anechOrEsen pros ten thalassan — and Jesus with his disciples withdrew toward the sea — anechOrEsen (withdrew, retreated) is the response to the death-plot of v.6: strategic withdrawal rather than direct confrontation. The movement toward the lake creates the outdoor teaching space of 4:1-2. Kai poly plEthos apo tes Galilaias EkolouthEsen — and a great multitude from Galilee followed — the following crowd is pan-regional: Galilee (v.7), Judea, Jerusalem, Idumea, Transjordan, Tyre and Sidon (v.8). The geographic spread is Markan staging for the universal scope of the mission.
3.8: Kai apo Ierosolymön kai apo tes Idoumaias kai peran tou Iordanou kai peri Tyron kai Sidona — and from Jerusalem and from Idumea and beyond the Jordan and around Tyre and Sidon — the geographic list deliberately spans Jewish and Gentile territory: Idumea (south of Judea, the homeland of the Herodians), Transjordan (east), and Tyre/Sidon (Phoenician cities of the northwest coast, Gentile territory). The anticipation of the Gentile mission is embedded in the crowd geography. AkOontes hosa poiei Elthan pros auton — hearing all he was doing, they came to him — fame (akouO = hearing) as the medium of approach, consistent with the akoe pattern of 1:28.
3.9: Kai eipen tois mathEtais autou hina ploiarion proskarte autO dia ton ochlon — and he told his disciples that a small boat should be ready for him because of the crowd — ploiarion (small boat, diminutive of ploion) is prepared as an escape valve: the crowd pressure was becoming physical (v.10). The boat enables Jesus to continue teaching at the lake without being overwhelmed (cf. 4:1 where the boat becomes the teaching platform). Hina mE thlibOsin auton — in order that they would not press him — thlibO (to press, crush, afflict) describes the physical crush of the crowd. Mark's vivid realism: the healer was in danger of being physically overwhelmed by those seeking healing.
3.10: Pollous gar etherapeuse, hOste epipiptein autO hina autou hapsOntai hosoi eichon mastigas — for he healed many, so that those who had diseases were pressing upon him in order to touch him — epipiptO (to fall upon, press heavily upon) describes the crowd's physical aggression: those with mastigas (scourges, plagues, afflictions — literally "whips," the word for the physical blows of a whipping; used metaphorically for illness) were pressing themselves against him. The connection between healing and touch is distinctive to Mark's physicality: the healings in ch.5 (woman with hemorrhage, 5:28-30; Jairus's daughter, 5:41) both involve touch. The crowd's instinct to touch him is not wrong.
3.11: Kai ta pneumata ta akatharta, hotan auton etheOroun, prosEpipton autO kai ekrazon legontes hoti Sy ei ho hyios tou theou — and the unclean spirits, whenever they saw him, fell before him and cried out saying "you are the Son of God" — the hotan + imperfect construction indicates recurring behavior: every encounter with unclean spirits produced the same response. ProsEpipton (fell before, prostrated themselves — the posture of submission before a superior power) + ekrazon (they cried out). The demonic recognition and involuntary submission is the third instance of the Messianic Secret pattern. The title ho hyios tou theou (the Son of God) is identical to the baptismal declaration of 1:11; demons know what disciples do not yet understand.
3.12: Kai polla epetima autois hina mE auton phaneron poiEsOsin — and he gave them many strict orders not to make him known/manifest — epitimaO (to charge/rebuke strictly, command with authority) + phaneron poiEsOsin (to make manifest, make publicly known). The Messianic Secret command here is the most explicit: the demons are ordered not to reveal him. The word phaneros (manifest, visible, publicly disclosed) is the disclosure term Mark uses at 4:22 for what will ultimately be revealed; the secret suppression is not permanent but a matter of timing and proper channel.
3.13: Kai anabainei eis to oros kai proskAleitai hous Ethelen autos — and he went up onto the mountain and summoned those whom he himself wanted — the ascent of the mountain is a Sinai-echo: the law-giver ascended the mountain; now the one with greater authority constitutes a new community. Hous Ethelen autos (those whom he himself wanted) — the sovereign, personal choice (autos emphatic) is the basis of the calling: they are chosen, not volunteers. Kai apElthon pros auton — and they came to him — the response matches the sovereign call: no resistance, no deliberation mentioned.
3.14: Kai epoiEsen dOdeka hina Osin met' autou — and he appointed/made twelve so that they might be with him — the founding purpose of the Twelve is stated first: hina Osin met' autou (in order that they might be with him) — the primary calling is not to do but to be with. The second and third purposes follow: kai hina apostellE autous kEryssein (to send them out to preach) and kai echein exousian ekballein ta daimonia (to have authority to cast out demons). The ordering matters: being with Jesus precedes and grounds the sending; presence is the source of the commission's authority.
3.15: Kai echein exousian ekballein ta daimonia — and to have authority to cast out demons — the three purposes of the Twelve appointment (v.14-15) match the three activities of Jesus's own ministry pattern: presence/relationship (v.14a), preaching (v.14b), exorcism (v.15). The Twelve are constituted as extensions of Jesus's own mission, carrying the same authority (exousia) that distinguished his teaching (1:22) and his commands to unclean spirits (1:27). The commissioning is a delegation of the Christological authority, not a separate source of power.
3.16: Kai epoiEsen tous dOdeka, kai epethEken onoma tO SimOni Petron — and he appointed the twelve, and he gave Simon the name Peter — epethEken onoma (placed/bestowed a name upon) — the act of naming carries authority: in the OT, God renames Abram/Abraham, Jacob/Israel; the one who names exercises sovereignty over the named. Petros (Peter) is the Greek transliteration of the Aramaic Kēphā (Cephas, rock/stone, 1 Cor 1:12; Gal 1:18). The renaming precedes the full commissioning of Peter's apostolic role; it is a prophetic act whose full significance becomes clear only in retrospect.
3.17: Kai Iakōbon ton tou Zebedaiou kai IOannEn ton adelphon tou Iakōbou, kai epethEken autois onomata BoanErges, ho estin huioi brontEs — and James the son of Zebedee and John the brother of James, and he gave them the name Boanerges, which is "sons of thunder" — BoanErges is a Greek transliteration of an Aramaic expression (possibly bney ragsha or bney re'em); Mark provides the Greek translation (huioi brontEs = sons of thunder). The name is preserved only in Mark and is not used elsewhere in the NT. The reason for the nickname is never explained; retrospectively, it may reflect the temperament displayed in Luke 9:54 (calling down fire) or the ambition of Mark 10:37 (requesting seats of honor). Mark uniquely preserves the Aramaic nickname alongside its translation.
3.18: Kai Andrean kai Philippon kai BartholomaIon kai Matthaion kai ThOman kai Iakōbon ton tou Alphaiou kai Thaddalon kai SimOna ton KananaIon — and Andrew and Philip and Bartholomew and Matthew and Thomas and James the son of Alphaeus and Thaddaeus and Simon the Cananaean — the list of apostles (cf. Matt 10:2-4; Luke 6:14-16; Acts 1:13) varies in ordering but shares the core Twelve. KananaIos (the Cananaean) is not "from Cana" but the Aramaic equivalent of the Greek ZElOtEs (Zealot, Luke 6:15; Acts 1:13), likely indicating association with or origin in a zealot movement. The list includes a tax collector (Levi/Matthew, v.14 of ch.2) and a zealot — political opposites in one apostolic community.
3.19: Kai Ioudan Iskariōth, hos kai paredōken auton — and Judas Iscariot, who also betrayed him — the final name on the list is the only one with an appended identification, and that identification is hos kai paredōken auton (who also betrayed him). The kai (also) is pointed: Judas was appointed to be with Jesus and to preach and to cast out demons — and he also betrayed him. The betrayal is noted at the moment of the appointment, not as an afterthought. Iskariōth may derive from ish-Keriyot (man of Kerioth, a Judean town), identifying Judas as the only non-Galilean among the Twelve.
3.20: Kai erchontai eis oikon, kai synerchetai palin ochlos, hOste mE dynasthai autous mEde arton phagein — and they entered a house, and the crowd came together again, so that they could not even eat bread — the domestic retreat is immediately overwhelmed. Mēde arton phagein (not even to eat bread) is a vivid detail of the extreme demand on Jesus: even the minimum physical sustenance is impossible. This detail recurs at 6:31 (oudE phagein eukairoun). The crowds' need is so constant that the normal rhythms of eating are disrupted. Mark's realism here — the press of the crowds does not allow even meals — gives the ministry a physical exhaustion dimension.
3.21: Kai akousantes hoi par' autou exElthon kratEsai auton, elegon gar hoti exestE — and those beside him, having heard, went out to seize him; for they said, "he is beside himself" — hoi par' autou (those beside him, those of his household) almost certainly refers to Jesus's family, confirmed by v.31's reference to his mother and brothers. KratEsai (to seize, take hold of by force — the same verb used for arresting Jesus in 14:1,44,46,49) is forceful: they intended to physically remove him. ExestE (he has gone out of himself, he is beside himself, out of his mind) — the family's diagnosis is that Jesus has lost his sanity; the religious authorities' diagnosis (v.22) is that he is demonized. Both interpretations of Jesus's behavior are rejected by the narrative.
3.22: Kai hoi grammateis hoi apo Ierosolymōn katabanteEs elegon hoti BeelZeboul echei kai hoti en tO archonti tOn daimonion ekballei ta daimonia — and the scribes who came down from Jerusalem were saying "he has Beelzebul" and "by the ruler of demons he casts out demons" — the Jerusalem delegation arriving in Galilee is significant: this is not a local Pharisaic grumbling but a formal evaluation sent from the religious capital. BeelZeboul (various spellings; possibly ba'al-zebul = lord of the high dwelling or lord of flies/filth, cf. 2 Kgs 1:2-3) is identified as the prince/ruler of demons (archonti tOn daimonion). The charge is the mirror-inverse of the truth: Jesus casts out demons by the Spirit of God (Matt 12:28); they attribute the same act to the demonic ruler.
3.23: Kai proskalesamenos autous en parabolais elegen autois, POs dynatai Satanas Satanan ekballein? — and having called them to him, he began to speak to them in parables: "how can Satan cast out Satan?" — proskalesamenos autous (having called them to him) — Jesus initiates the debate by drawing his accusers into proximity; he does not avoid the charge but responds directly. The parabolic counter-argument begins with an internal-coherence question: an exorcist who serves Satan would be self-defeating. Satanas Satanan ekballein — Satan casting out Satan — the logical contradiction of the accusation is stated in the most compressed form: the premise makes the action structurally impossible.
3.24: Kai ean basileia eph' heautEn meristhE, ou dynatai stathEnai hE basileia ekeinE — and if a kingdom is divided against itself, that kingdom cannot stand — the argument from analogy: a divided kingdom is structurally self-defeating. Meristhē (divided, distributed, parceled out — from merizO) applied to a kingdom: a realm at war with itself cannot sustain its own coherence. The application to the exorcism charge: if Jesus's exorcisms are Satanic acts, Satan is destroying his own domain. The internal coherence of the demonic realm (however disordered) would be undermined by casting out its own members.
3.25: Kai ean oikia eph' heautEn meristhE, ou dynEsetai hE oikia ekeinE stathEnai — and if a house is divided against itself, that house cannot stand — the household (smaller than a kingdom) provides the second analogical level: domestic division produces structural collapse. The household image is immediate and observable; every hearer knows that a family that fights against itself destroys itself. The double analogy (kingdom, v.24; household, v.25) establishes the principle at both scales before the application to the demonic realm in v.26.
3.26: Kai ei ho Satanas anestE eph' heauton kai emeristhE, ou dynatai stathEnai alla telos echei — and if Satan has risen against himself and is divided, he cannot stand but has come to an end — the application of the analogy to Satan directly: if the exorcisms represent Satan acting against himself, it means his end (telos echei = has an end, is finished). The Christological implication: that Satan's kingdom is in fact falling is correct, but the reason is not internal division but the arrival of the stronger one (cf. 1:7: ho ischyroteros mou) who binds him from outside.
3.27: All' ou dynatai oudeis eis ten oikian tou ischyrou eiselthOn ta skeue autou diarpasai, ean mE prOton ton ischyron dEsE kai tote ten oikian autou diarpasei — but no one can enter the house of the strong man and plunder his goods unless first he binds the strong man; and then he will plunder his house — the strong-man parable is the Christological key to the entire Beelzebul controversy. HO ischyros (the strong man) = Satan; Jesus is the one who has first bound him (prOton ton ischyron dEsE), making the exorcisms possible. The exorcisms are not signs of demonic alliance but signs of demonic defeat: Jesus plunders the strong man's house because he has already bound the strong man. The binding may refer to the wilderness temptation (1:13) or to the eschatological power of the kingdom.
3.29: Hos d' an blasphEmEsE eis to pneuma to hagion, ouk echei aphesin eis ton aiOna, alla enochos estin aiOniou hamartEmatos — but whoever blasphemes against the Holy Spirit does not have forgiveness unto the age, but is guilty of an eternal sin — aiOniou hamartEmatos (eternal sin/offense — aiOnios = of the age, pertaining to the coming age, age-long) is unique in the NT. The standard translation "unforgivable sin" accurately captures the practical meaning (ouk echei aphesin eis ton aiOna = does not have forgiveness for the age) but the term itself is eternal sin, emphasizing its age-to-come consequences. The sin is the attribution of the Holy Spirit's works (exorcism) to the demonic — a permanent inversion that places oneself outside the Spirit's reach who would grant repentance.
3.30: Hoti elegon, Pneuma akatharton echei — because they were saying "he has an unclean spirit" — v.30 is Mark's editorial explanation of why Jesus spoke the unforgivable-sin saying: it was a direct response to the specific charge that he was possessed by an unclean spirit. The explanation clarifies the referent of "blasphemy against the Holy Spirit": it is specifically the inversion of Spirit-empowerment and demonic possession — claiming that the Holy Spirit's exorcistic work is instead the work of an unclean spirit. This is not a general category of sin but a very specific theological inversion.
3.31: Kai erchontai hE mEtEr autou kai hoi adelphoi autou kai exO stEkontes apesteilan pros auton kalountes auton — and his mother and his brothers came and standing outside they sent word to him, calling him — the family arrives (cf. v.21 where they went out to seize him). HExO stEkontes (standing outside) is spatially significant: Jesus's physical family is outside the house; the crowd is inside. The narrative has already set up the redefinition of family in vv.33-35. Hoi adelphoi autou (his brothers) — the natural reading is biological siblings; the alternative (cousins, or children of Joseph from a previous marriage) is theologically motivated but grammatically strained.
3.32: Kai ekatheto peri auton ochlos, kai legousin autO, Idou hE mEtEr sou kai hoi adelphoi sou exO zEtousin se — and a crowd was sitting around him, and they said to him: "look, your mother and your brothers are outside seeking you" — ekatheto peri auton (was sitting in a circle around him) — the crowd physically surrounds Jesus in the posture of disciples receiving teaching. This spatial arrangement will become the answer to the question in v.33: the circle around him is the definition of his family. ZEtousin se (seeking you) — the same verb used in 1:37 (everyone seeks you!) and in 3:32 and will be used of those who seek Jesus's death. The usage here is neutral but carries the ambiguous charge of the word.
3.33: Kai apokritheis autois legei, Tis estin hE mEtEr mou E hoi adelphoi mou? — and answering them he said: "who is my mother and who are my brothers?" — the rhetorical question (tis estin = who is?) sets up the redefinition. The question is not a denial of biological relationship but a relativization of it: the biological bond, however real, is not the primary or ultimate category of kinship. Jesus will answer with a gesture (v.34) and a declarative statement (v.35) that redefines family on the basis of shared orientation toward the Father's will rather than biological descent.
3.34: Kai periblepsamenos tous peri auton kuklO kathEmenous legei, Ide hE mEtEr mou kai hoi adelphoi mou — and looking around at those sitting in a circle around him he said: "here are my mother and my brothers" — periblepsamenos (having looked around — the Markan characteristic scanning gaze, also at 3:5; 5:32; 9:8; 10:23) with kuklO (in a circle) — the same word as the crowd's seating arrangement in v.32. The gestural answer: Ide (Look! Behold!) pointing to the seated disciples. The ones gathered around him in the posture of receiving teaching are identified as his mother and brothers — a community of belonging constituted by presence with Jesus and attentiveness to his word.
3.35: Hos an poiEsE to thelEma tou theou, houtos adelphos mou kai adelphE kai mEtEr estin — whoever does the will of God, that one is my brother and sister and mother — the addition of adelphE (sister) to the male-focused terms brother and mother signals the inclusive scope of the new family. Poieo to thelEma tou theou (does the will of God) is the defining criterion: not biological descent, not ethnic identity, not religious observance as such, but active alignment with the Father's purpose. The same criterion appears in Matthew's Gospel in a different context (7:21; 12:50); here it defines not entry into the kingdom but membership in the family of Jesus.

4.11: hymin to mysterion dedotai tes basileias tou theou — to you the mystery of the kingdom of God has been given — mysterion (mystery) in Jewish apocalyptic usage is the hidden divine plan that is disclosed only to the elect (Dan 2:18, 27-30; 1QpHab 7). The disclosure is a gift (dedotai, divine passive) not an achievement. ekeinois de tois exo en parabolais ta panta ginetai — but for those outside everything is in parables — the binary (inside/outside) reflects the community's self-understanding as those who have received the revelation that the world has not; the parables function simultaneously as revelation (for insiders) and concealment (for outsiders who refuse).
4.26: hos anthropos bale ton sporon epi tes ges kai katheude kai egeire nykta kai hemeran kai ho sporos blastan kai mekynetai — the kingdom of God is as if a man scatters seed on the ground and sleeps and rises night and day and the seed sprouts and grows he knows not how — the unique feature of this Markan-only parable is the farmer's passivity: the growth occurs automatē (v.28, of itself, automatically, the origin of our word automatic). The kingdom's growth is not a human program but a divine process; human beings plant and wait but do not control the growth.
4.1: Kai palin Erxato didaskein para ten thalassan — and again he began to teach beside the sea — palin (again) connects to the lakeshore teaching of 2:13; the outdoor setting by the lake is established as a regular venue. Kai synagetai pros auton ochlos pleistos, hOste auton eis ploion emBanta kathEsthai en te thalasse — and the largest crowd yet gathered to him, so that he got into a boat and sat in the sea — the superlative pleistos (very large, greatest crowd) signals escalation. The boat-as-podium removes Jesus from the pressing crowd (cf. 3:9) while allowing him to address everyone on the shore. The spatial arrangement — speaker in the boat, audience on the beach — creates an amphitheater effect, the water carrying his voice.
4.2: Kai edidAsken autous en parabolais polla — and he was teaching them many things in parables — the imperfect edidasken (was teaching, ongoing) with polla (many things) establishes the parabolic teaching as a sustained mode, not a single occasion. Parabolē (parable — from para + ballō, to throw alongside) is the placing of two things side by side for comparison: the earthly scene (farming, fishing, domestic life) placed alongside the kingdom reality to illumine it. Kai elegen autois en te didachE autou — and he was saying to them in his teaching — the editorial transition signals that a representative parable follows.
4.3: AkouEte — Listen! — the imperative opening is the parable's own call for the kind of attention that will yield understanding. The same verb akouO (to hear) appears at v.9 (ho echōn Ōta akouein akouetō = whoever has ears to hear let him hear) and v.12 (the Isaiah hearing-without-understanding quotation). The entire parable section is framed by the call to hear and the paradox of hearing without understanding. Idou exElthen ho speirOn speirai — behold a sower went out to sow — idou (behold) invites visual imagination. The historic present exElthen makes the scene immediate. The sower's identity is left entirely ambiguous until the interpretation in v.14 (the sower sows the word).
4.4: Kai egeneto en tO speirein ho men epesen para ten hodon — and as he sowed, some fell beside the road — the fourfold soil sequence begins. Para ten hodon (beside/along the road) — the packed earth of a footpath through or beside the field: impenetrable to the seed, which lies on the surface. Kai Elthen ta peteina kai katephagen auto — and the birds came and ate it up — the immediate consumption leaves no trace of the seed's landing. The interpretation (v.15) identifies the birds with Satan who removes the word before it can take root.
4.5: Kai allo epesen epi to petrOdes — and other seed fell on the rocky ground — petrOdes (rocky, full of rock — from petra, rock/bedrock) is soil with a thin layer over bedrock, not soil full of scattered stones. Hopou ouk eichen gen pollen, kai euthys exanEtEilen dia to mE echein bathos gEs — where it did not have much soil, and immediately it sprang up because it had no depth of soil — the speed of germination (euthys) is caused by the shallowness: without the need to penetrate deeply, the seed springs up quickly. The quick appearance is deceptive — the depth required for sustaining the plant is absent.
4.6: Kai hote anEteilen ho Elios ekauma tIsthE kai dia to mE echein rizan exEranthE — and when the sun rose it was scorched, and because it had no root it dried up — ekauma tIsthE (was scorched — kaumatizO from kauma, burning heat) and exEranthE (was dried up — the same root xErainO as the withered hand of 3:1). The absence of root (rizan) is the lethal condition: the apparent vitality of v.5 (springing up quickly) masks the structural deficiency. The interpretation (v.17) identifies this with those who respond with joy but have no root and fall away under persecution.
4.7: Kai allo epesen eis tas akanthas kai anebEsan hai akanthai kai synEpnixan auto kai karpon ouk edOken — and other seed fell into the thorns and the thorns came up and choked it and it did not give grain — synEpnixan (choked, strangled — from sympnigO, to choke together) describes the thorns growing up simultaneously with the grain and crowding it out. Karpon ouk edOken (gave no fruit/grain) is the diagnostic outcome: growth occurred but the productive end was prevented. The interpretation (v.19) identifies the thorns with the worries of the age, the deceitfulness of wealth, and desires for other things.
4.8: Kai alla epesen eis ten gen ten kalEn kai ediDou karpon anabainonta kai auxanomena kai epheren en triakonta kai en hexEkonta kai en hekaton — and other seed fell into the good soil and was giving fruit, coming up and growing, and was bearing thirty and sixty and a hundredfold — the good soil produces triple gradations of yield: 30, 60, 100. The imperfect ediDou (was giving, kept giving) indicates sustained fruitfulness rather than a single harvest. The yields are remarkable by ancient agricultural standards (typical yields were 5-10 fold); the 30/60/100 is not uniformly extraordinary but varies — even the lowest good-soil yield is notable. The interpretation (v.20) simply names the outcome: they hear, accept, and produce a crop.
4.9: Kai elegen, Ho echOn Ōta akouein akouetO — and he was saying: "whoever has ears to hear let him hear" — the hearing-call concludes the parable and picks up the opening imperative of v.3 (akouete). The idiom ho echOn Ōta (having ears) distinguishes the capacity for hearing from the mere anatomical possession of ears: the point is that spiritual perception is a gift or condition, not automatic. The reflexive structure (akouein akouetO — hearing/let-him-hear) intensifies: the one who can hear should really hear. The same formula appears in 4:23 and throughout the Gospels and Revelation (2:7,11,17,29 etc.).
4.10: Kai hote egeneto kata monas, ErOtOn auton hoi peri auton syn tois dOdeka tas parabolas — and when he was alone, those around him along with the Twelve were asking him about the parables — kata monas (alone, apart — the idiom for private withdrawal from the public) creates the inner-circle setting for the explanation. The questioners are broader than just the Twelve: hoi peri auton (those around him) is the wider circle of committed followers, with the Twelve as the core. They ask about tas parabolas (the parables, plural) — not just the sower parable but the entire parabolic teaching mode. The question occasions the explanation of the parabolic method (vv.11-12) before the specific sower-interpretation (vv.13-20).
4.12: Hina blepOntes blepOsin kai mE idOsin, kai akOuontes akOuOsin kai mE syniOsin, mEpote epistrepsOsin kai aphethe autois — in order that seeing they may see and not perceive, and hearing they may hear and not understand, lest they should turn and be forgiven — the hina (in order that) introduces a purpose clause citing Isa 6:9-10 — one of the most theologically difficult verses in the Gospels. The hardening purpose of parables: the parables are not merely pedagogy for the slow but vehicles of concealment for those who have placed themselves outside. Three proposed readings: (1) the hina is genuinely purposive (God's sovereign hardening, cf. Rom 9-11); (2) the hina translates an Aramaic de- that can be either purpose or result; (3) the context qualifies the hardening as God's response to existing rejection. The Isaianic echo is intentional: Mark's Jesus stands in the line of Isaiah, whose proclamation both revealed and concealed.
4.13: Kai legei autois, Ouk oidate tEn parabolEn tautEn, kai pOs pasas tas parabolas gnOsesthe? — and he said to them: "do you not know this parable? then how will you know all the parables?" — OuK oidate (do you not know/understand) implies the disciples' failure to grasp the parable is a failure that calls for explanation. The meta-question — "if you don't get this parable, how will you understand any parable?" — establishes the sower parable as foundational: it is the key parable, the one whose interpretive logic (hearing the word, response types, fruit or no fruit) governs all the others. Understanding this parable is prerequisite to understanding the parabolic method as a whole.
4.14: Ho speirOn ton logon speirei — the sower sows the word — the interpretation's first move is the identification of the seed: ton logon (the word, the gospel message). The sower's identity remains ambiguous — Jesus, the disciples, any preacher. The interpretive focus falls entirely on the soil (the receiver-types), not on the sower. Ton logon is the technical term for the early Christian message (Acts 4:4; 6:2,4; 8:4; 1 Thess 1:6), indicating that the parable was read in the early church as a map of mission-experience: why does the same proclamation produce such different results?
4.15: Houtoi de eisin hoi para ten hodon hopou speiretai ho logos, kai hotan akOusOsin, euthys erchetai ho Satanas kai airei ton logon ton esparmenon eis autous — these are the ones along the road where the word is sown; and when they hear, immediately Satan comes and takes away the word that was sown in them — euthys erchetai ho Satanas (immediately Satan comes) — the euthys of demonic activity mirrors the euthys of Jesus's own activity. The immediacy of removal before the word can take root is the mechanism of the first soil's failure: the word never gets the chance to germinate. The Satanic taking-away is a forcible removal (airei = takes up, removes), not a gradual fading.
4.16: Kai houtoi eisin homoiOs hoi epi ta petrOdE speiromenoi, hoi hotan akOusOsin ton logon euthys meta charas lambanousin auton — and these likewise are the ones sown on the rocky places, who when they hear the word immediately receive it with joy — the joy-reception (meta charas lambanousin auton) is the initial positive response that makes the rocky-ground hearer the most deceptive soil-type. The outward response is genuinely positive: immediate, joyful reception. Euthys again — the same immediacy that marks both Satanic removal (v.15) and the quick germination of shallow soil (v.5). Speed without depth is the danger.
